why: Fun and fairness are human-perception judgments. Whether grinding feels FAIR, whether the snap reads as TRUSTWORTHY rather than mysterious magnetism, whether bails are readable, catching feels skillful, and steering feels intuitive cannot be established by synthetic ContactFrame tests or green goldens (final-observability §1: fun/fair gates require human formative testing, never claimed from synthetic runs). Grinds are the milestone most at risk of the product owner's warning that 'it's very easy to make a version of this game that's difficult and confusing and broken' — so M6 pauses for a formative human pass (n >= 5) before M9 builds scoring/HUD on top.
user_actions: 0. PREREQUISITE (render): confirm the two grindable obstacles are VISIBLE in the playable build. The rail COLLIDERS + descriptors exist in the sim (see notes), but drawing their meshes is a render/* task outside M6 — grinding an invisible rail would itself fail the fairness bar (research/physics-and-game-feel §6.4: 'Camera hides rail -> approach errors'). Do not run the human test until the ledge (+X side) and rail (-X side) are rendered.
1. From the repo root double-click play.bat (or run it from a terminal). It builds the game bundle + the native WebView2 host and launches with your REAL trackpad. Plant TWO fingers to ride.

2. Recruit at least 5 players (mix of skate-game familiarity). Each plays 20-30 minutes with light framing only ('cruise, ollie, and try to grind the rail/ledge'). Do NOT coach the grind entry — trust is exactly what we are measuring.

3. Have each player deliberately attempt grinds: steer toward a side obstacle, ollie onto it, ride it, and hop or slip off. Try both the low wide ledge and the higher thin rail. Try at assist levels 0, 1, 2 (default 1).

4. After each session, the player fills the survey in README.md (5 core questions + free text). Capture: does grinding feel FAIR? Is the snap TRUSTWORTHY or MYSTERIOUS (did the board ever grind when you didn't mean to, or refuse to grind when you lined it up)? Are bails/slips READABLE (did you understand why you came off)? Does catching feel SKILLFUL? Is steering INTUITIVE?
5. Note any moment a player said 'why did it grind?' or 'why won't it grind?' or 'that felt unfair' — verbatim, with what they were doing. These are the magnetism / invisible-snap failure signals.
6. Aggregate the >= 5 responses into survey-summary.md in this folder (per-question distribution + the verbatim frustration quotes + a pass/revise verdict).
continue_when: survey-summary.md exists with n >= 5 and shows: grinding reads as FAIR and the snap as TRUSTWORTHY (no unexplained latches or refusals in the majority of sessions), slips/bails are READABLE, and the overall feel matches the 'mindlessly kick-flipping and grinding back in time to childhood' Tech-Deck stress-reliever vision. If players report mysterious magnetism, invisible snap, 'why won't it grind', or death-loop frustration, RESUME by retuning GrindConfig (candidateVolumeRadius / rSnap / balance gains / envelopes) and re-run the formative pass — do not proceed to M9 on a failing verdict.
resume_commit: HEAD @ feat(m6): grinds — 50-50 + boardslide (the commit that adds this packet). Base was e26758f.
notes: Rails are physics + data, not yet rendered. Geometry is exported for the render layer to consume WITHOUT any new API: packages/game/src/sim/levels/flat-dev.ts exports FLAT_DEV_LEDGE (wide forgiving ledge, +X side, top y=0.15) and FLAT_DEV_RAIL (thin rail, -X side, top y=0.4), both along +Z from z=2..30. The player steers left/right off the default +Z cruise line to reach them.

Fairness knobs for the retune loop, all in shared/config.ts GrindConfig (doc-commented, hypothesis-labelled): candidateVolumeRadius (how early the candidate pip shows), rSnap[L] (the tight lateral+vertical geometric-contact gate — the anti-magnetism axis; L0 ~0 = pure physics, L2 most forgiving), latchLateralSpring[L] (soft-snap firmness), balanceLimit / cleanBalanceBand / balanceInputGain / balanceSelfCenter (survive vs clean vs counter-lean authority; self-centre keeps it a no-death-loop), fiftyFiftyEnvelopeDeg / boardslideEnvelopeDeg (approach-angle envelopes), vMin/vMax (speed window), speedEndSpeed (dismount speed).

What the HUD (M9) must render to make the snap visible: ObserveState.grind = { active, family, balance, candidate }. candidate === true is the 'in the snap volume, plausible approach' pip (show it BEFORE the latch — the whole point). balance in [-balanceLimit, balanceLimit] is the meter (|balance| < cleanBalanceBand = clean/scoring). Telemetry grindCandidate carries acuteDeg/relSpeed/lateralDist/heightDelta for a debug/approach overlay.
Assist level is a human-set profile choice (not auto): default 1 (soft snap). L0 = pure physics (must land dead-on, no spring); L2 = stronger snap + wider rSnap. Ask players which level felt fairest.

Boardslide entry is the delicate one and IS proven end-to-end (grind-lab boardslide golden + integration test): a gentle nose-prep steer yaws the board ~50° off the rail, the approach-align completes the rotation toward perpendicular as it descends, and the LOW-FRICTION rail lets the deck SLIDE (min-combined 0.1, so 50-50 trucks are unchanged) so it keeps speed and rides instead of deck-slamming to a stop. The G2 humans should judge whether this entry FEELS learnable on a real trackpad — the input mapping for a ~90° boardslide setup is the likeliest thing to need a feel pass.

This packet claims NOTHING about fun/fair on its own — it only defines the human protocol. The synthetic evidence (37 automated grind tests across 6 files: detection + envelope rejection, visible candidate, forgiving balance + no-death-loop, geometric-contact/no-teleport, phase-exclusivity, yaw-alignment ride physics incl. a boardslide-converges simulation, 50-50 + boardslide record/replay goldens, random-approach property) proves the system is DETERMINISTIC and STRUCTURALLY FAIR, which is necessary but not sufficient; the human pass is the sufficiency check.
